title: Build a board of stakeholders and let them tear your strategy apart first
teaser: Multi-persona prompting: have AI play the people in the room, so you hear the objections before the meeting.
steps: Pick your board. Two or three people who'd push back hardest, and what each one cares about.
Load them into a project. Drop the context and personas into Claude or ChatGPT (prompt below). Tell each to name itself before it answers.
Run them one at a time. Pass each answer to the next so they react to each other. Watch where they disagree.
body:
Ever walked into a meeting and watched someone raise a flag you never saw coming? Multi-persona prompting lets you hear it first. You build a small board of the people who'll be in the room, hand it to AI, and let them argue over your strategy before the real ones do.

Example board, for a factory training rollout:

  • Operations lead — wants output, hates anything that stops the line.
  • Compliance officer — wants everything documented and defensible.
  • Instructional designer — wants people to actually retain it.

Where it falls apart: vague personas. They blur into one polite voice that agrees with everything, which feels like it worked but didn't. If nobody disagrees, sharpen what each one wants. The disagreement is the whole point.
prompt_body:
You are a roundtable of [N] stakeholders reviewing a [strategy / plan / design]. Here is the context:

[Paste your strategy and the situation.]

The board:
- [Stakeholder 1]: cares most about [priority]. Pushes back when [X].
- [Stakeholder 2]: cares most about [priority]. Pushes back when [Y].
- [Stakeholder 3]: cares most about [priority]. Pushes back when [Z].

Rules:
- Critique and build on this together, but NOT all at once.
- Each stakeholder names themselves before they speak.
- Respond as ONE stakeholder per turn. I'll prompt you for the next.
- Disagree where you genuinely would. Don't smooth it over.

Start with [Stakeholder 1].

title: Make AI interview you about the problem
teaser: Before you ask AI for answers, get it to ask you better questions about the problem.
body:
Most people open ChatGPT and ask for a needs analysis template. That's the wrong first move. The fastest way to build a real understanding of a training need is to have AI interview you — playing the role of a sharp consultant who refuses to start designing until the brief is honest.

Why this works:

  • It forces you to articulate what you actually know and don't know, in your own words.
  • It surfaces the assumptions you'd otherwise smuggle into the design.
  • It gives you a transcript you can paste into a discovery brief, share with stakeholders, or come back to in a week.

How to use it: Paste the prompt below into Claude or ChatGPT. Answer the questions one at a time — don't batch them. When the AI is satisfied with your answers, ask it to summarize what it heard into a one-page discovery brief.

What it won't do: Replace real stakeholder interviews. This is for clarifying your own understanding before you go in — or after you come out, to stress-test what you heard.
prompt_body:
You are a senior learning consultant. I'm about to design a training program and I want you to make sure I actually understand the problem before I start.

Interview me one question at a time. Don't move on until I give you a real answer — push back if I'm vague, hand-wavy, or jumping to solutions.

Cover, in this order:

1. The performance gap — what should people be doing that they aren't, and how do you know?
2. The audience — who are they, what do they already know, what's their motivation level?
3. The constraints — time, budget, modality preferences, political sensitivities.
4. The success criteria — how will we know this worked? What changes for the business?
5. The risks — what's the most likely way this training fails to land?

When you're satisfied with my answers, summarize them as a one-page discovery brief in clear, no-jargon language.

Ready? Ask your first question.
